title: Ubiquiti UniFi Network Application Path Traversal CVE-2026-22557 (CVSS 10.0): Unauthenticated Account Takeover Threatens 88,000 Internet-Exposed Controllers — Chained with NoSQL Injection CVE-2026-22558 for Full Admin Compromise
affected_products: Ubiquiti UniFi Network Application Official Release 10.1.85 and earlier
Ubiquiti UniFi Network Application Release Candidate 10.2.93 and earlier
Ubiquiti UniFi Express Firmware 4.0.13 and earlier (includes application 9.0.114 and earlier)

timeline.event: Ubiquiti quietly releases UniFi Network Application version 10.1.89 with security fixes
Ubiquiti publishes Security Advisory Bulletin 062 disclosing CVE-2026-22557 (CVSS 10.0) and CVE-2026-22558. Researcher n00r3 (@izn0u) credited with discovery
Censys reports approximately 87,000-88,000 UniFi Network Application instances publicly exposed; 28,000+ in the United States. BleepingComputer and CyberScoop report vulnerability
Government CERTs including Canadian Centre for Cyber Security and Singapore CSA issue security advisories; multiple security research blogs publish technical analysis
CVE-2026-22559 (third vulnerability in same advisory, CVSS 8.8) published on NVD
Not yet in CISA KEV; no confirmed active exploitation publicly reported; detection rules published on GitHub; exploitation expected imminently given low complexity

simple_summary.what_happened: Ubiquiti, maker of the widely-deployed UniFi network equipment management platform, disclosed a perfect-score critical vulnerability on March 18, 2026. The flaw allows any attacker on the internet to take complete control of a UniFi controller — the central management system for all network devices — without needing any login credentials. The bug is a 'path traversal' flaw, meaning an attacker can trick the server into reading and writing files it shouldn't have access to, including the credential database. This is the third maximum-severity vulnerability Ubiquiti has disclosed in the past year. Patches were released March 17-18, 2026, but nearly 88,000 controllers remain publicly accessible on the internet.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations and individuals running Ubiquiti UniFi Network Application version 10.1.85 or earlier, or UniFi Express with firmware 4.0.13 or earlier, exposed to the internet. UniFi is widely deployed across enterprises, universities, hotels, hospitals, managed service providers, and small businesses to manage Wi-Fi access points, network switches, and security gateways. Censys identified approximately 88,000 internet-exposed instances globally.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker identifies internet-exposed UniFi Network Application controllers (88,000+ targets reachable on default port 8443) using Shodan, Censys, or similar tools. Step 2: Without any authentication, attacker sends crafted HTTP requests containing path traversal sequences (../) to the UniFi application. Step 3: The server fails to properly validate the file paths, allowing the attacker to read sensitive system files including the credential database, authentication tokens, and configuration files. Step 4: Attacker extracts administrator credentials or authentication tokens, gaining full access to the UniFi controller management interface. Step 5: Attacker optionally chains with CVE-2026-22558 (NoSQL injection) to escalate privileges to full administrator and then modifies network device configurations, creates backdoor admin accounts, or intercepts network traffic across the entire managed network infrastructure.
what_you_should_do: Upgrade UniFi Network Application to version 10.1.89 or later immediately
Restrict access to UniFi controller port 8443 to internal management networks only — remove all internet-facing exposure
Review UniFi controller audit logs for unexpected access or configuration changes since March 17, 2026
Rotate all UniFi admin credentials and invalidate active sessions after patching
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: The UniFi controller is the single point of control for an organization's entire network infrastructure. Compromising it gives attackers the ability to intercept all network traffic, redirect DNS, manage every connected device, and create persistent network-level backdoors. With 88,000 controllers exposed on the internet, the attack surface is enormous. Path traversal attacks require almost no technical sophistication, and the attack complexity rating is LOW — meaning commodity attackers can exploit this without specialized skills. The combination with NoSQL injection (CVE-2026-22558) for privilege escalation makes this a complete, devastating attack chain.

tactics.description: Attackers exploit CVE-2026-22557 path traversal via unauthenticated HTTP requests to the UniFi Network Application to access credential files and achieve account takeover
Path traversal allows reading the UniFi credential database and configuration files containing hashed or plaintext administrator credentials and authentication tokens
CVE-2026-22558 NoSQL injection allows an authenticated attacker to escalate privileges to full administrator within the UniFi management interface
Attackers create backdoor administrator accounts in the UniFi controller to maintain persistent access to the network management plane
Through the compromised UniFi controller, attackers manage and modify firmware/configurations on all connected network devices (access points, switches, gateways)
With full controller access, attackers can enable port mirroring, modify traffic routing, or modify DNS settings to intercept and collect network traffic from all devices managed by the controller

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on HTTP logs for path traversal patterns and filesystem access anomalies. The community has released detection rules for Sigma/SIEM, KQL (Sentinel), Splunk, and YARA. Monitor port 8443 traffic and controller admin logs for unauthorized access.

log_sources.description: Monitor HTTP requests to the UniFi management port (default 8443) for path traversal sequences (../, %2e%2e%2f, %252e%252e%252f) in request URI or parameters
Review admin audit events for new admin account creation, configuration changes, password resets, or device firmware updates by unexpected users or from unexpected IP addresses
Monitor inbound connections to port 8443 from external IPs; any unauthenticated external access to this port should be treated as suspicious
Alert on unexpected modifications to UniFi database files, configuration directories, or credential stores

what_to_look_for.indicator: Path traversal sequences in HTTP requests to port 8443
what_to_look_for.description: HTTP requests containing ../, %2e%2e%2f, or URL-encoded variants targeting the UniFi application indicate active exploitation attempts
what_to_look_for.log_field: cs-uri CONTAINS '../' OR cs-uri CONTAINS '%2e%2e%2f' AND cs-port='8443'

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unauthenticated requests to sensitive UniFi API endpoints
what_to_look_for.description: Requests to credential store, configuration, or database endpoints without valid session cookies from external IPs
what_to_look_for.log_field: cs-method='GET' AND cs-uri CONTAINS '/api/' AND cs-cookie='-' AND cs-ip NOT IN internal_ranges

what_to_look_for.description: Creation of new super-admin or admin accounts in the UniFi controller, especially from IPs not previously associated with administration
what_to_look_for.log_field: audit_event='Admin Added' OR audit_event='Role Changed to Super Admin'

what_to_look_for.indicator: Bulk configuration changes across multiple network devices
what_to_look_for.description: Mass firmware updates, configuration pushes, or network policy modifications applied to multiple devices simultaneously — potential post-exploitation activity
what_to_look_for.log_field: audit_event='Device Firmware Updated' AND device_count > 5 AND time_window < 300s

immediate_actions.action: Upgrade UniFi Network Application to version 10.1.89 or later
immediate_actions.description: Immediately upgrade all UniFi Network Application instances to version 10.1.89 (or 10.2.97 for release candidates). For UniFi Express devices, update firmware to 4.0.13 or later. Self-hosted instances require manual update; UniFi Cloud Key devices may auto-update.

immediate_actions.action: Remove UniFi controller from internet-facing exposure
immediate_actions.description: Restrict access to UniFi controller port 8443 to internal management networks or VPN only using firewall rules. No legitimate use case requires direct internet exposure of the UniFi management interface.
immediate_actions.command:
# Block external access to UniFi port 8443 (example iptables rule):
iptables -I INPUT -p tcp --dport 8443 ! -s 10.0.0.0/8 -j DROP
# Or restrict in your perimeter firewall/security group

immediate_actions.action: Audit controller for unauthorized access and rotate all credentials
immediate_actions.description: Review UniFi controller admin logs for any access from external IPs, especially between March 17-27, 2026. Rotate all admin passwords, invalidate all active sessions, and review connected device configurations for unauthorized changes.

immediate_actions.action: Deploy detection rules for path traversal attempts
immediate_actions.description: Install available community detection rules from the GitHub repository (GarethMSheldon/cve-2026-22557-unifi-detection) which provides Sigma rules, KQL queries for Microsoft Sentinel, Splunk SPL searches, and YARA rules for HTTP log scanning.
